caseStudies.title: Case Study — SolarHub Project (Question 4b)
caseStudies.text:
You are a project manager at Global Innovations Corp., a leading technology firm specialising in sustainable energy solutions. Your current project, "SolarHub," aims to design and install solar panels in a remote village. The project is sponsored by an environmental non-profit organisation and has received media coverage.

The SolarHub project has a duration of 6 months and is currently in its fourth month, with a Planned Value (PV) of $100,000. As of last week, the project's Actual Cost (AC) is $120,000, and the Earned Value (EV) stands at $80,000.

The project scope includes 10 deliverables and 8 milestones. The project has a risk register with 15 identified risks and a contingency reserve of $10,000. The project has a stakeholder register with 20 stakeholders and a communication plan with 5 communication channels.

This case study applies to Question 4b.

questions.label: Question 1a
questions.text: The project management process groups are initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and ______.
questions.explanation: The five PMBOK Process Groups are: Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing. The Closing process group formally ends the project or phase, including obtaining final acceptance, archiving records, and releasing resources.

questions.label: Question 1b
questions.text: A vision statement is a document that describes the desired future state of an organisation or a project in terms of its ______, values, and purpose.
questions.explanation: A vision statement articulates the desired future state of an organisation or project in terms of its aspirations (long-term goals), values (guiding principles), and purpose (reason for existence). 'Goals' or 'mission' may also be accepted.

questions.label: Question 1c
questions.text: A SWOT analysis is a technique that assesses the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and ______ of an organisation or a project.
questions.explanation: SWOT stands for Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats. Strengths and weaknesses are internal factors; opportunities and threats are external factors. SWOT is commonly used during project initiation and strategic planning.

questions.label: Question 1d
questions.text: A project estimate is an assessment of the likely amount of resources that will be consumed by the project based on the available ______, assumptions, and constraints.
questions.explanation: Project estimates are based on available information (e.g., scope details, historical data, expert knowledge), assumptions (what is presumed to be true), and constraints (limiting factors such as budget or time). The quality of estimates depends heavily on the quality and completeness of the available information.

questions.label: Question 1e
questions.text: A network diagram is a graphical representation of the project activities and their ______ relationships.
questions.explanation: A project network diagram (using the Activity-on-Node / Precedence Diagramming Method) shows activities as nodes and arrows to represent precedence (dependency) relationships — i.e., which activities must be completed before others can start. 'Dependency' or 'logical' are also acceptable answers.

questions.label: Question 1f
questions.text: The critical path is the longest sequence of activities in a project network diagram that determines the ______ duration of the project.
questions.explanation: The critical path is the longest path through the project network and determines the minimum possible project duration. Any delay to an activity on the critical path directly extends the project end date. Activities on the critical path have zero total float.

questions.label: Question 1g
questions.text: A risk ______ is a document that records the identified risks, their causes, impacts, probabilities, responses, and current status.
questions.explanation: A Risk Register is a key project document used to record all identified risks along with their causes, likelihood, potential impact, assigned owners, planned response strategies, and current status. It is created during risk identification and updated throughout the project lifecycle.

questions.label: Question 1h
questions.text: Earned value management (EVM) is a technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to measure the project ______.
questions.explanation: EVM integrates the three dimensions of project management — scope (what was planned), schedule (when it was planned), and cost (what was budgeted) — to objectively measure project performance at any point in time. Key metrics include CV, SV, CPI, and SPI.

questions.label: Question 1i
questions.text: A scrum is a popular framework for implementing agile project management that organises the project into short iterations called ______.
questions.explanation: In Scrum, work is organised into time-boxed iterations called Sprints (typically 1–4 weeks). At the end of each Sprint, the team delivers a potentially shippable product increment. Scrum events include Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective.

questions.label: Question 1j
questions.text: A ______ team is a role in scrum that consists of cross-functional members who work together to deliver potentially shippable increments of the product at the end of each sprint.
questions.explanation: The Development Team (also called the Scrum Team's developers) is a self-organising, cross-functional group responsible for doing the work of delivering a potentially shippable product increment each Sprint. The three Scrum roles are: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team.

questions.label: Question 2
questions.text: Apply the principles of project management to answer the following questions on project organisational structures.

subQuestions.label: Question 2a
subQuestions.text: Define project organisational structure and explain its purpose for project management.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
DEFINITION OF PROJECT ORGANISATIONAL STRUCTURE:
A project organisational structure is the formal arrangement that defines how authority, responsibility, communication, and resources are allocated and coordinated among individuals and teams involved in a project. It describes the hierarchical or functional relationship between the project manager, project team members, and the broader organisation.

PURPOSE FOR PROJECT MANAGEMENT:

1. Clarity of Authority and Accountability
The organisational structure establishes who has authority to make decisions, who is accountable for results, and how power flows between the project manager and functional managers. Without a clear structure, authority conflicts can paralyse decision-making.

2. Resource Allocation and Control
Different structures determine how resources (people, budget, equipment) are allocated to the project. For example, in a projectised structure, the PM has full control over resources, while in a functional structure, the functional manager retains resource authority. Clear structure ensures the right people are assigned and available.

3. Communication Channels
Organisational structure determines how information flows — vertically (up and down the hierarchy) and horizontally (across departments). A well-defined structure reduces communication gaps, prevents information silos, and ensures stakeholders receive timely updates.

4. Role and Responsibility Definition
The structure supports the development of tools like the RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed), ensuring every project role and task has a clearly identified owner and avoiding duplication of effort or gaps in responsibility.

5. Alignment with Organisational Goals
The structure ensures the project is integrated into the broader organisational context, allowing senior management to monitor progress, escalate issues, and align project outcomes with strategic objectives.

In summary, a project organisational structure is the backbone of project governance — it provides the framework within which the project manager leads the team, manages resources, and delivers results on time and within budget.
keyPoints: Define project organisational structure clearly — formal arrangement of authority, responsibility, and communication
Explain at least 4 distinct purposes: authority/accountability, resource allocation, communication, role definition
Link organisational structure to the project manager's effectiveness
Mention how structure affects stakeholder management and reporting
Note that structure must align project work with organisational strategy

subQuestions.label: Question 2b
subQuestions.text: Describe the THREE (3) main types of project organisational structures and give an example of each type.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
THREE MAIN TYPES OF PROJECT ORGANISATIONAL STRUCTURES:

1. FUNCTIONAL STRUCTURE
Description: The project is organised within the existing functional departments of the organisation (e.g., Engineering, Finance, Marketing). Each team member reports to their functional manager, not to a dedicated project manager. The project manager (if one exists) has limited authority and acts primarily as a coordinator.
Characteristics: Functional managers control resources and priorities; project work is secondary to departmental work; team members may work on multiple projects simultaneously.
Advantages: Strong technical expertise within departments; clear career paths for team members; efficient use of specialists.
Disadvantages: Weak project focus; project manager has little authority; slow decision-making due to departmental silos.
Example: A university IT department upgrading its student management software using existing IT staff who report to the Head of IT — not a dedicated project manager.

2. PROJECTISED (PROJECT-BASED) STRUCTURE
Description: The organisation is structured around projects. Team members are assigned full-time to the project and report directly to the project manager, who has complete authority over resources, budget, and decision-making.
Characteristics: Strong PM authority; dedicated project teams; team members may lack a permanent 'home' department.
Advantages: Clear accountability; fast decision-making; strong project identity and team cohesion.
Disadvantages: Resource duplication across projects; team members may feel insecure about their post-project placement; expensive to maintain.
Example: A construction company organising its workforce entirely around building projects, where each project has its own dedicated project manager, engineers, and support staff.

3. MATRIX STRUCTURE
Description: A hybrid structure where team members report to both their functional manager AND a project manager simultaneously. The balance of authority varies from Weak Matrix (functional manager dominant) to Strong Matrix (project manager dominant) to Balanced Matrix (shared authority).
Characteristics: Dual reporting lines; better resource sharing across projects; requires strong communication and negotiation skills.
Advantages: Efficient resource utilisation; access to functional expertise; stronger project focus than functional structure.
Disadvantages: Dual authority can create conflict and role ambiguity; requires clear escalation procedures; team members may experience stress from competing priorities.
Example: A pharmaceutical company developing a new drug where the project manager leads the cross-functional team (scientists, regulatory affairs, marketing) while each member still reports to their respective department head.
keyPoints: Describe all three types: Functional, Projectised, Matrix
For each: describe key characteristics, PM authority level, and resource control
Provide a specific, realistic example for each type
Mention the sub-types of matrix (Weak, Balanced, Strong) for completeness
Identify at least one advantage and one disadvantage per type

subQuestions.label: Question 2c
subQuestions.text: Demonstrate how different types of organisational structures affect the role and skills of the project manager.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
HOW ORGANISATIONAL STRUCTURE AFFECTS THE PM'S ROLE AND SKILLS:

1. FUNCTIONAL STRUCTURE — Coordinator/Influencer Role
In a functional structure, the project manager has minimal formal authority. Resources are controlled by functional managers, not the PM. The PM must rely heavily on influence, negotiation, and interpersonal skills rather than formal power. Key skills required: stakeholder management, political awareness, persuasion, and the ability to achieve results without direct authority. The PM's role is closer to a project coordinator — gathering information, facilitating communication, and escalating issues to functional managers.

2. PROJECTISED STRUCTURE — Leader/Decision-Maker Role
In a projectised structure, the PM has full authority over the team and resources. The PM functions as a true project leader — making independent decisions on budget, schedule, scope, and team management. Skills required include: leadership, technical acumen, financial management, conflict resolution, and full accountability for project outcomes. The PM's role is comprehensive and entrepreneurial — they operate almost like a business unit manager.

3. MATRIX STRUCTURE — Negotiator/Integrator Role
In a matrix structure, the PM shares authority with functional managers and must navigate dual reporting lines. The PM must be highly skilled in conflict management, negotiation, and cross-functional collaboration. The risk of role ambiguity requires the PM to establish clear agreements with functional managers on resource availability and priorities. Skills required: matrix management, diplomatic communication, formal authority over project tasks but not over people, and the ability to motivate team members who do not directly report to them.

CONCLUSION:
The structure fundamentally shapes the PM's power base. As the structure shifts from functional to matrix to projectised, the PM gains progressively more authority and autonomy — but also greater accountability. Effective PMs adapt their leadership style to the structure they operate within.
keyPoints: Address all three structure types and how each changes the PM's role
Functional: PM is a coordinator, relies on influence not authority
Projectised: PM has full authority and accountability
Matrix: PM must negotiate with functional managers — dual authority challenge
Identify specific skills needed for each structure type
Conclude that PM style must adapt to the structure

questions.label: Question 3
questions.text: Apply the principles of project management to answer the following questions on project costs and cost estimation.

subQuestions.label: Question 3a
subQuestions.text: Define the following terms: project costs, direct costs, project direct overhead costs, and G&A costs.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
DEFINITIONS OF PROJECT COST TERMS:

1. PROJECT COSTS
Project costs refer to the total financial expenditure required to complete the project — encompassing all resources consumed from initiation to closure. This includes labour, materials, equipment, subcontractors, facilities, and any other resource with a monetary value. Project costs are captured in the project budget and tracked against the cost baseline throughout the project lifecycle. They include both direct and indirect costs.

2. DIRECT COSTS
Direct costs are expenses that can be specifically and exclusively attributed to a particular project or work package. They are traceable directly to the project without any need for cost allocation. Examples include:
- Labour costs for project team members working exclusively on the project
- Materials purchased solely for the project (e.g., construction materials, equipment)
- Subcontractor fees for work performed specifically for the project
- Travel expenses incurred directly for project purposes
Direct costs are typically the first costs estimated and tracked in a project budget.

3. PROJECT DIRECT OVERHEAD COSTS
Project direct overhead costs (also called project overhead or project indirect costs) are costs that are indirectly caused by the project but cannot be attributed to a single work package or task. They are necessary for the project to proceed but support multiple activities simultaneously. Examples include:
- Project management office (PMO) support allocated to the project
- Shared equipment or facility costs (e.g., site office rental, shared tools)
- Project-level insurance and licensing fees
- Project-specific administrative support
These costs are allocated to the project as a whole rather than to individual tasks.

4. G&A COSTS (General and Administrative Costs)
G&A costs are overhead costs incurred at the organisational level that are not directly attributable to any single project but are necessary for the overall functioning of the organisation. They are allocated across multiple projects or departments based on an overhead rate or cost allocation formula. Examples include:
- Executive management salaries
- Corporate accounting, HR, and legal departments
- Head office rent and utilities
- Organisational IT infrastructure
G&A costs are typically expressed as a percentage of direct costs or labour and are added on top of the project's direct and indirect costs to determine the total project price.
keyPoints: Define all four terms clearly and distinctly
Project costs = total financial expenditure (direct + indirect)
Direct costs = exclusively and directly attributable to the project (with examples)
Project direct overhead = indirectly caused by the project, shared across multiple project activities (with examples)
G&A costs = organisational-level overhead, not attributable to any single project (with examples)
Demonstrate understanding of the hierarchy: project costs > direct costs + overhead + G&A

subQuestions.label: Question 3b
subQuestions.text: Outline TWO (2) methods of estimating project costs and compare their advantages and disadvantages.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
TWO METHODS OF ESTIMATING PROJECT COSTS:

METHOD 1: ANALOGOUS ESTIMATING (Top-Down Estimating)
Definition: Analogous estimating uses historical cost data from similar past projects to estimate the cost of the current project. The estimator identifies a comparable completed project and adjusts the estimate based on known differences in scope, complexity, or scale.

Advantages:
- Quick and inexpensive to produce — does not require detailed project scope
- Useful in early project phases (initiation or feasibility) when limited information is available
- Leverages organisational knowledge and institutional memory of past projects
- Provides a rough order-of-magnitude estimate suitable for initial budget approval

Disadvantages:
- Lower accuracy — typically ±25–50% or worse, depending on how similar the reference project is
- Relies heavily on the availability and reliability of historical project data
- Risk of systematic bias if the reference project was itself over/under budget
- May overlook unique aspects of the current project that increase costs significantly

Best Use: Early in the project lifecycle when speed matters more than precision, or when detailed scope is not yet defined.

METHOD 2: BOTTOM-UP ESTIMATING
Definition: Bottom-up estimating involves decomposing the project scope (via the WBS) into the smallest work packages, estimating the cost of each individual package, and aggregating (rolling up) all estimates to arrive at the total project cost.

Advantages:
- Highest accuracy of all estimating methods — typically ±5–10% when done well
- Forces thorough planning and analysis of all project work
- Creates clear cost accountability at the work package level, making tracking and control easier
- Engages the people who will do the work in the estimation process, improving buy-in and realism

Disadvantages:
- Time-consuming and expensive to produce — requires a detailed WBS and significant estimating effort
- Only practical in later planning stages when the full scope is defined
- Risk of omissions or padding at the work package level — team members may overestimate to protect themselves
- Not suitable for early feasibility studies when scope is still undefined

Best Use: During detailed project planning for complex, large-scale projects where budget precision is critical (e.g., construction, IT system implementation).

COMPARISON SUMMARY:
| Aspect            | Analogous Estimating     | Bottom-Up Estimating        |
|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Accuracy          | Low (±25–50%)            | High (±5–10%)               |
| Time required     | Fast                     | Slow                        |
| Cost to produce   | Low                      | High                        |
| Requires detail   | Minimal                  | Detailed WBS required       |
| Best project phase| Initiation / Feasibility | Planning / Execution        |
keyPoints: Name both methods clearly (Analogous / Top-Down; Bottom-Up)
Define each method in plain language
At least two advantages AND two disadvantages for each method
Include an accuracy comparison (Analogous = rough; Bottom-Up = precise)
State when each method is most appropriate in the project lifecycle
A comparison table or summary adds structure and clarity

subQuestions.label: Question 3c
subQuestions.text: Identify TWO (2) sources of uncertainty or risk that can affect project cost estimation. Show how they can be managed or mitigated.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
TWO SOURCES OF UNCERTAINTY AFFECTING PROJECT COST ESTIMATION:

SOURCE 1: SCOPE UNCERTAINTY (Incomplete or Evolving Requirements)
Description: When the project scope is not fully defined at the time of estimation, cost estimates will be incomplete or inaccurate. As requirements evolve (scope creep) or new work packages are discovered, costs escalate beyond the original estimate. This is particularly common in IT and software development projects where user requirements are difficult to pin down at the outset.

Impact on Cost Estimation: If 20% of the project scope is undiscovered at estimation time, the resulting budget will be at least 20% below the final actual cost. Scope changes also disrupt cost baselines, making variance analysis meaningless.

Management/Mitigation Strategies:
- Invest in thorough scope definition and sign-off before cost estimation begins. Use a detailed WBS to ensure all work is captured.
- Implement a formal Change Control Process — all scope changes must be assessed for cost impact and approved before work proceeds. This prevents unbudgeted additions.
- Add a contingency reserve (typically 5–15% of project budget) to cover known unknowns — foreseeable risks that could add cost.
- Use progressive elaboration — estimate in phases as more scope information becomes available, refining the estimate as the project progresses.

SOURCE 2: EXTERNAL PRICE VOLATILITY (Market and Commodity Risk)
Description: The cost of resources — particularly materials, labour, and subcontracted services — can fluctuate significantly between the time of estimation and the time of procurement. For long-duration or large-scale projects, commodity price changes (steel, concrete, fuel) or labour market shifts (skill shortages driving up wage rates) can dramatically inflate actual costs above estimates.

Impact on Cost Estimation: A project estimated in year one may face 10–20% higher material costs by year two due to inflation, supply chain disruptions, or exchange rate movements. In construction, a 10% rise in steel prices on a large infrastructure project can result in millions of dollars of cost overrun.

Management/Mitigation Strategies:
- Use price escalation factors in estimates — include an inflation or price escalation allowance based on economic forecasts for the project duration.
- Include price escalation clauses in procurement contracts — or negotiate fixed-price contracts with suppliers to transfer the price risk to the vendor.
- Conduct market analysis before estimation to use current pricing data, not stale historical data.
- Maintain a management reserve (separate from contingency reserve) to cover unknown unknowns, including unexpected market movements.
- For long-duration projects, adopt phased procurement — commit to purchasing materials closer to when they are needed, reducing the window of price exposure.
keyPoints: Identify two clearly distinct sources of cost estimation risk (scope and external price are ideal; alternatives: poor estimating assumptions, resource availability, technology changes)
Explain the impact of each source on cost accuracy
At least two specific, actionable mitigation strategies per source
Distinguish between contingency reserve (known unknowns) and management reserve (unknown unknowns)
Link mitigation strategies to real PM tools: WBS, change control, escalation clauses, progressive elaboration

questions.label: Question 4
questions.text: Apply the principles of project management to answer the following questions on project closure and earned value management.

subQuestions.label: Question 4a
subQuestions.text: Define the term project closure and explain its purpose and importance.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
DEFINITION OF PROJECT CLOSURE:
Project closure (also called project closeout) is the final phase of the project management lifecycle in which all project activities are formally completed, deliverables are handed over to the client or operations team, resources are released, and the project is officially ended. It applies both to successfully completed projects and to projects that are terminated early.

PURPOSE OF PROJECT CLOSURE:

1. Formal Acceptance of Deliverables
Project closure ensures that the client or sponsor formally accepts and signs off on all project deliverables and that the agreed scope has been fully met. This transfers ownership of the deliverable from the project team to the customer, fulfilling the contractual obligation.

2. Release of Resources
Once the project is closed, human resources, equipment, and budget are formally released and made available for other projects or operational activities. Without a formal closure, resources may continue to be charged to the project unnecessarily, inflating final costs.

3. Financial Close-Out
All invoices, purchase orders, and contracts are finalised and settled. The final project cost is reconciled against the budget, and any unspent contingency reserves are returned to the organisation.

4. Lessons Learned Capture
Project closure is the primary opportunity to document what went well, what went wrong, and what should be done differently in future projects. Lessons learned are recorded in a Lessons Learned Register and archived as an Organisational Process Asset (OPA), contributing to continuous improvement across the organisation.

5. Administrative Closure
All project documentation — plans, reports, contracts, designs, and records — is archived in an organised and accessible manner for future reference, audit, or legal purposes.

IMPORTANCE OF PROJECT CLOSURE:
- Prevents resource leakage: Without formal closure, team members may continue working on tasks that are no longer required, wasting time and budget.
- Ensures contractual compliance: Formal client sign-off protects the organisation from future disputes about whether deliverables were accepted.
- Supports organisational learning: Lessons learned from each project improve the organisation's collective knowledge, leading to better performance on future projects.
- Maintains stakeholder trust: A well-executed closure demonstrates professionalism, reinforces the project manager's credibility, and strengthens the client relationship for future engagements.
- Enables clean transition: For projects delivering new systems or infrastructure, closure ensures a smooth handover to operations, including training documentation and support arrangements.

In summary, project closure is not merely an administrative formality — it is an essential phase that protects the organisation legally and financially, preserves project knowledge, and sets the foundation for future success.
keyPoints: Clear definition of project closure — formal ending of the project
Explain at least 4 purposes: formal acceptance, resource release, financial close-out, lessons learned, administrative closure
Explain importance — prevent resource leakage, contractual compliance, organisational learning, stakeholder trust
Mention lessons learned as a key output that feeds future projects
Note that closure applies to both completed AND terminated projects

subQuestions.label: Question 4b
subQuestions.text:
Using the concept of Earned Value Management (EVM), determine the following metrics for the SolarHub project (refer to the case study):

(i) Schedule Variance (SV)
(ii) Cost Variance (CV)
(iii) Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
(iv) Cost Performance Index (CPI)

Show your calculations and interpret your results in detail. Comment on the project in terms of its budget and schedule. Explain if this project can be considered to be on track.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
GIVEN DATA (from SolarHub case study):
- Planned Value (PV) = $100,000
- Earned Value (EV) = $80,000
- Actual Cost (AC) = $120,000

(i) SCHEDULE VARIANCE (SV)
Formula: SV = EV − PV
SV = $80,000 − $100,000
SV = −$20,000

Interpretation: The SV is NEGATIVE (−$20,000), which means the SolarHub project is BEHIND SCHEDULE. Only $80,000 worth of planned work has been completed when $100,000 worth should have been done by now. The project has completed only 80% of its planned work to date.

(ii) COST VARIANCE (CV)
Formula: CV = EV − AC
CV = $80,000 − $120,000
CV = −$40,000

Interpretation: The CV is NEGATIVE (−$40,000), which means the project is OVER BUDGET by $40,000. The team has spent $120,000 to produce only $80,000 worth of value. For every dollar of work delivered, $1.50 was actually spent.

(iii) SCHEDULE PERFORMANCE INDEX (SPI)
Formula: SPI = EV ÷ PV
SPI = $80,000 ÷ $100,000
SPI = 0.80

Interpretation: SPI < 1.0 means the project is BEHIND SCHEDULE. The team is progressing at only 80% of the planned rate — delivering 80 cents of planned work for every dollar's worth of work that should have been completed. At this rate, a 6-month project may take approximately 7.5 months (6 ÷ 0.80).

(iv) COST PERFORMANCE INDEX (CPI)
Formula: CPI = EV ÷ AC
CPI = $80,000 ÷ $120,000
CPI = 0.667 (approximately 0.67)

Interpretation: CPI < 1.0 means the project is COST INEFFICIENT. For every $1.00 spent, only $0.667 of value is being delivered. Alternatively, to complete $1 worth of work, the project is spending $1.50 — a 50% cost overrun per unit of value.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT — IS THE PROJECT ON TRACK?
The SolarHub project is NOT on track. It is performing poorly on BOTH dimensions:

- Schedule: SPI = 0.80 — the project is 20% behind its planned progress. At the current rate, the 6-month project will likely take approximately 7.5 months, missing its November deadline.
- Cost: CPI = 0.667 — the project is spending 50% more per unit of work than planned. Without corrective action, the final cost will significantly exceed any original budget.

Immediate corrective actions should include:
1. Investigating the root causes of the cost overrun (over-spending on labour, materials, or both)
2. Fast-tracking or crashing schedule-critical activities to recover lost time
3. Re-forecasting the Estimate at Completion (EAC = BAC ÷ CPI) and reporting the revised forecast to the project sponsor
4. Reviewing risk reserves — the $10,000 contingency may already be partially consumed
keyPoints: SV = EV − PV = $80,000 − $100,000 = −$20,000 (must show formula)
CV = EV − AC = $80,000 − $120,000 = −$40,000 (must show formula)
Both SPI < 1 AND CPI < 1 — project is behind schedule AND over budget
Project is NOT on track — clear explanation referencing both metrics
Suggest at least one corrective action
